source.title: Beyond Borders: Why the Muslim World Needs a Unified Digital Payments System
source.summary: The Fragmentation ProblemWhen a Malaysian entrepreneur wants to purchase textiles from an Indonesian supplier, or when a Saudi charity seeks to transfer funds to a relief organization in Bangladesh,...
source.author: Guest Contributor
source.body_text: When a Malaysian entrepreneur wants to purchase textiles from an Indonesian supplier, or when a Saudi charity seeks to transfer funds to a relief organization in Bangladesh, they encounter a labyrinth of intermediaries, currency conversions, and transaction fees that can consume up to 7% of the transfer value. This inefficiency stands in stark contrast to the seamless digital payment systems emerging within individual nations, and represents a missed opportunity for the 1.8 billion Muslims across 57 Organization of Islamic Cooperation (OIC) member states. The World Bank estimates that remittances to OIC countries exceeded $400 billion in 2023, with intra-OIC trade valued at approximately $600 billion annually. Yet these massive flows remain shackled by fragmented payment infrastructures that rely heavily on Western-dominated networks like SWIFT, Visa, and Mastercard. The average cost of sending remittances within OIC countries stands at 6.8%, nearly double the United Nations Sustainable Development Goal target of 3%. This represents an annual drain of over $27 billion; resources that could otherwise fuel economic development, poverty alleviation, and entrepreneurship across the Muslim world. The case for a unified OIC digital payments infrastructure extends beyond mere economics into the realm of Islamic principle. The Quran explicitly encourages cooperation among believers: "And cooperate in righteousness and piety, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression" (5:2). Economic integration that facilitates halal commerce, reduces exploitation through excessive fees, and strengthens the collective capacity of Muslim nations represents a contemporary application of this divine directive. The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) established the first Islamic state in Medina with a unified market system that eliminated arbitrary barriers to trade among diverse tribes. His prohibition of najash (artificial price inflation) and his emphasis on transparency in commercial transactions provide timeless principles for modern payment systems. A unified digital infrastructure could embed these Shariah-compliant principles at the architectural level, ensuring that every transaction across the Muslim world adheres to Islamic commercial ethics. Moreover, the Islamic concept of the ummah; the global community of believers; calls for practical solidarity beyond rhetorical…
